content.text: Why the Platform Needs a Roadmap

An observability platform without a roadmap is permanently reactive: the team spends all its time responding to tenant requests, fighting fires caused by cardinality explosions, and keeping up with upstream version upgrades. Product teams have no visibility into what capabilities are coming, so they build workarounds (shadow Prometheus instances, hand-rolled log parsers) that duplicate effort and fragment the platform.

A published roadmap with a governance process changes the relationship between the platform team and its stakeholders. Product teams submit feature requests through a structured channel, the platform team evaluates and prioritises them, and stakeholders can see the queue. The process creates mutual accountability: the platform team commits to a delivery timeline, and product teams commit to adopting new capabilities rather than building their own.

attrs.alt: Platform Governance Cadence and Decision Authority Matrix

content.text: The platform roadmap operates at three horizons simultaneously. The
(current quarter) contains committed work with named engineers and delivery dates. The
(next quarter) contains planned work that is scoped but not yet assigned. The
(6–12 months out) contains directional bets and technology evaluations that are subject to change based on usage data and stakeholder input.

# docs/observability-platform/roadmap.yaml
# Published to the platform team's wiki page. Updated quarterly.

team: observability-platform
last_updated: "2024-Q3"
next_review: "2024-10-01"

now:  # Q3 2024 — committed
  - id: PLAT-201
    title: "Upgrade Loki to 3.1 for per-stream retention"
    owner: "@infra-alice"
    eta: "2024-08-15"
    motivation: "Enables per-team retention without separate Loki instances"
    tenant_impact: "Zero-downtime rolling upgrade; no client changes required"

  - id: PLAT-202
    title: "Ship genai-otel-sdk 1.4.0 with gen_ai.cost_usd attribute"
    owner: "@sdk-bob"
    eta: "2024-09-01"
    motivation: "Real-time cost attribution per request (RFC-042 accepted)"
    tenant_impact: "Opt-in in 1.4.0, default-on in 1.5.0 (Q4)"

  - id: PLAT-203
    title: "Add cardinality enforcement to collector CI gate"
    owner: "@dx-carol"
    eta: "2024-09-15"
    motivation: "Prevent high-cardinality labels from reaching Prometheus"
    tenant_impact: "New CI check; teams with violations get 30d grace period"

next:  # Q4 2024 — planned
  - id: PLAT-210
    title: "Adaptive tail sampling based on model error rate"
    owner: TBD
    eta: "2024-12-01"
    motivation: "Auto-increase sampling rate when a model's error rate spikes"
    depends_on: [PLAT-201]

  - id: PLAT-211
    title: "Cost anomaly alert as a standard platform alert"
    owner: TBD
    eta: "2024-11-15"
    motivation: "Every tenant gets cost spike alerting without manual setup"

later:  # 2025 — directional
  - id: PLAT-220
    title: "Evaluate OpenTelemetry Semantic Conventions 1.25+ for LLM"
    motivation: "OTel LLM semconv is stabilising; align gen_ai.* with upstream"
    status: "Monitoring upstream; target adoption H1 2025"

  - id: PLAT-221
    title: "Unified cost + quality dashboard for GenAI program leadership"
    motivation: "Executive-facing view aggregating all GenAI spend and quality KPIs"
    status: "Requirements gathering with FinOps team"
The RFC Process for Platform Changes

Changes that affect all tenants — new required labels, breaking SDK changes, storage backend migrations — require an RFC (Request for Comments) to go through a structured review before implementation. The RFC process gives product teams advance notice, surfaces implementation concerns early, and creates a paper trail for why decisions were made.
Document answers: problem, proposal, impact on tenants, rollback plan
At least 3 stakeholder comments; all blocking concerns listed
Accept, Reject, or Defer with written rationale
The platform lead uses these criteria when deciding on an RFC, in priority order:
does this introduce or eliminate a compliance risk? Security RFCs are fast-tracked.
does this change the cardinality, throughput, or storage cost profile in a way that risks SLO violation?
how many tenants benefit, and how significantly? A change that unblocks 10 teams beats one that benefits 1.
what is the engineering cost, and does it fit within the platform team's capacity?
does this move toward or away from the platform's long-term direction (e.g., OTel standardisation)?

# RFC-NNN: [Short Title]

**Status:** Draft | Open | Accepted | Rejected | Deferred
**Author:** @github-handle
**Created:** YYYY-MM-DD
**Decision date:** YYYY-MM-DD (filled when status changes from Open)
**Implements roadmap item:** PLAT-NNN (if applicable)

## Problem Statement
<!-- What is the current state and why is it insufficient? Include data. -->

## Proposed Change
<!-- What specifically changes? Include config diffs or API changes. -->

## Impact on Tenants
<!-- What do tenants need to do (if anything)? What breaks if they don't migrate?
     What is the migration timeline? -->

## Rollback Plan
<!-- How do we undo this if it causes problems in production? -->

## Alternatives Considered
<!-- What else was evaluated and why was this option chosen? -->

## Open Questions
<!-- Outstanding concerns that commenters should address. -->

---
## Comments
<!-- Stakeholders add comments here before the decision date. -->
The platform team maintains two communication channels: a
for past changes (what shipped, when, what tenants need to do) and a
sent monthly to all engineering team leads summarising platform health, upcoming changes, and adoption metrics.

# Observability Platform Changelog

## 2024-09-15 — genai-otel-sdk 1.4.0

**What changed:** Added `gen_ai.cost_usd` span attribute (opt-in).
**Why:** RFC-042 accepted. Enables real-time cost attribution per request.
**Action required:** Update SDK dependency. No breaking changes.
**Default-on in:** 1.5.0 (scheduled 2024-11-01). You have 6 weeks to migrate.
**Migration guide:** https://wiki.example.com/observability/sdk/1.4-migration

## 2024-08-15 — Loki upgraded to 3.1

**What changed:** Per-stream retention now enabled. Each Loki tenant can have
its own retention period without sharing a global TTL.
**Why:** PLAT-201. Required for GDPR storage limitation compliance.
**Action required:** None. Retention periods already configured per your intake form.
**Incident risk window:** Rolling upgrade ran 2024-08-14 14:00–16:30 UTC.
No data loss; 3 tenants saw 5-minute query gaps (post-mortem: PLAT-PM-041).

# Observability Platform — August 2024 Update

## Platform Health
- Ingest availability: 99.97% (SLO: 99.9%) ✅
- Loki query P95: 1.8s (SLO: <3s) ✅
- Open cardinality violations: 3 teams (down from 7 last month)

## What Shipped
- Loki 3.1 upgrade (per-stream retention live)
- Cardinality CI gate now enforced (30-day grace period started Aug 1)

## What's Coming in September
- genai-otel-sdk 1.4.0 with gen_ai.cost_usd (Sept 1)
- Cardinality enforcement becomes blocking (grace period ends Sept 30)

## Action Required This Month
- Teams with cardinality violations: see PLAT-203 migration guide
- All teams: update SDK before Sept 30 for cost dashboards to populate

## Usage Highlights
- 12 tenants onboarded (up from 9)
- Total ingest: 4.2M spans/day, 18GB logs/day
- Highest-cost tenant: genai-search (340K spans/day)

The observability platform consumes real compute and storage resources that must be budgeted and attributed. Without cost transparency, the platform team has no leverage to push back on tenants who generate disproportionate ingest volume, and engineering leadership has no way to evaluate whether the platform investment is justified.

This cost breakdown is shared with engineering leadership in the quarterly roadmap review. Teams that consistently generate more than 25% of total platform cost receive a capacity review conversation — not a bill, but an opportunity to discuss whether their ingest volume is justified and whether sampling could reduce it without losing signal.

The platform team tracks two types of delivery metrics: output metrics (did we ship what we committed to?) and outcome metrics (did shipping it improve things for tenants?). Output metrics are necessary for accountability; outcome metrics are what actually matter.
Roadmap items delivered on time (committed quarter)
Onboarding intake date vs dashboard merge date
SDK adoption rate (% services with gen_ai.* attrs)
Mean time to tenant resolution (platform-caused incidents)
Anonymous survey to all engineering teams

Tenant satisfaction surveys are often overlooked in platform teams because the feedback feels vague compared to metrics. They are the only mechanism that surfaces problems the platform team cannot see in their own dashboards — for example, that the SDK documentation is confusing, that the onboarding process requires too many tickets, or that a specific Grafana feature the team relies on broke after an upgrade and nobody filed a bug report.

The observability platform roadmap establishes three horizons of commitment (now/next/later) and four governance cadences (weekly sync, monthly tenant review, quarterly roadmap review, annual strategy). Significant platform changes go through an RFC process that gives product teams five business days to comment before a decision is made. Cost transparency — publishing per-tenant ingest cost monthly — creates accountability without billing internal teams. Delivery is tracked against both output metrics (roadmap items shipped on time, RFC cycle time) and outcome metrics (time-to-first-dashboard, SDK adoption rate, tenant satisfaction). Together, these mechanisms turn the observability platform from a shared utility that everyone relies on and nobody maintains into a product with an owner, a roadmap, and a measurable value proposition.
